sanskrit_devanagari:
हतो वा प्राप्स्यसि स्वर्गं जित्वा वा भोक्ष्यसे महीम् ।
तस्मादुत्तिष्ठ कौन्तेय युद्धाय कृतनिश्चयः ॥ ३७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
hato vā prāpsyasi svargaṁ jitvā vā bhokṣyase mahīm |
tasmād uttiṣṭha kaunteya yuddhāya kṛta-niścayaḥ || 37 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase renders 'svargaṁ' as 'स्वर्ग' without final anusvara — known display variant; we adopt 'स्वर्गं' with anusvara per holy-bhagavad-gita and gitasupersite)
exact (IAST text body identical; plain transliteration without diacritics)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.037 = MBh_06,024.037). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: O son of Kuntī, either you will be killed on the battlefield and attain the heavenly planets, or you will conquer and enjoy the earthly kingdom. Therefore, get up with determination and fight.

If you fight, you will either be slain on the battlefield and go to the celestial abodes, or you will gain victory and enjoy the kingdom on earth. Therefore arise with determination, O son of Kunti, and be prepared to fight.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Even though there was no certainty of victory for Arjuna's side, he still had to fight; for, even being killed there, he could be elevated to the heavenly planets.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.33-37 Atha ca etc., upto krta-niscavah. Accepting what the opponent has stated, this pentad of verses is narrated as an argument: 'If your goodself prefers to abide by the generally accepted practice, even then this [fighting] must be undertaken necessarily'.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara groups 2.33-2.37 as a single argumentative pentad. The structure: even granting Arjuna's worldly framing (kṣatriya honor, public esteem, fear of dishonor), the conclusion is the same — the fight must be undertaken. 2.37 closes the pentad with the symmetric-outcome resolution: slain → heaven; victorious → earth. Both are wins, in the framework Arjuna himself accepts. The verse uses 'tasmāt' (therefore) to pull the conclusion of the disgrace argument forward and the imperative 'uttiṣṭha' (arise) to make the conclusion an action, not a doctrine. Shankara reads this as a concession argument: the real argument is the metaphysical one (2.11-2.30); 2.31-2.37 is the worldly-frame argument given as additional support.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.37 If you are slain in a righteous war by enemies, you shall thereby attain supreme bliss. Or, slaying the enemies, you shall enjoy this kingdom without obstacles. As the duty called war, when done without attachment to the fruits, becomes the means for winning supreme bliss, you will attain that supreme bliss.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja gives 2.37 a niṣkāma-karma reading already — anticipating the explicit doctrine in 2.38-2.47. The duty (yuddha) becomes the means to liberation when performed without attachment to the fruits. Both outcomes (slain or victorious) become forms of supreme bliss when the action is done from this stance. The verse is therefore not just a 'both outcomes win' argument; it is a foreshadowing of the karma-yoga doctrine that 2.38 will state explicitly. Ramanuja's reading anchors 2.37 forward into the karma-yoga block rather than backward into the disgrace argument.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The previous statement given by Arjuna in Chapter 2, verse six, about his concern of not knowing which is better: to conquer the enemy or be conquered by the enemy, is now being clarified by the Supreme Lords instruction in this verse, that by either conquering he will win dominion of the earth or by being conquered he will directly attain the heavenly spheres.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Sridhara explicitly links 2.37 back to Arjuna's own original confusion at 2.6 ('I do not know which is better — that we conquer them or they conquer us'). 2.37 is the closure of that question: Krishna answers 2.6 directly, 30 verses later. Both branches of Arjuna's stated dilemma are wins. The dilemma was not actual; Arjuna constructed it under fear.

positions.rendering: either you will be killed on the battlefield and attain the heavenly planets
If you fight, you will either be slain on the battlefield and go to the celestial abodes
concession argument — granting Arjuna's worldly framing, both outcomes are wins (slain → heaven; victorious → earth)
niṣkāma-karma foreshadow — the duty done without attachment to fruits becomes the means to liberation in either outcome
direct answer to Arjuna's own dilemma at 2.6: both branches he stated are wins

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators agree on the surface: slain → heaven, victorious → earth. They differ on what argumentative work the verse is doing. Shankara reads it as the closing of the worldly-frame concession (granting Arjuna's own value system, the conclusion is the same). Ramanuja reads it as the bridge into karma-yoga — the symmetry only holds because the action is done without attachment. Sridhara reads it as the direct closure of Arjuna's own 2.6 dilemma. The literal translation is uncontroversial; the doctrinal scope of the symmetric-outcome claim is what the commentators sharpen.

literal_meaning: Slain, you will obtain heaven; or, victorious, you will enjoy the earth. Therefore, arise, son of Kuntī, with firm resolve to fight.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The closing verse of the worldly-frame argument (Shankara's pentad 2.33-2.37) and the pivot to the karma-yoga discipline that 2.38 will name explicitly. The argument's structure: 2.31 establishes svadharma; 2.32-2.36 close every escape route from svadharma (a kṣatriya offered righteous battle that is the open gate to heaven; failure to fight means dharma loss, sin, ill-fame; ill-fame outlasts death; the formerly esteemed will rewrite you as cowardly; your enemies will speak indecent words denigrating your prowess). 2.37 then resolves: both outcomes of fighting (slain or victorious) are wins in the framework Arjuna himself accepts. The dilemma Arjuna constructed at 2.6 is not real — both branches lead forward. Therefore, arise. The 'tasmāt uttiṣṭha kaunteya yuddhāya kṛta-niścayaḥ' is the imperative culmination of the worldly-frame argument. The pivotal commentarial split: Shankara reads the verse as concession (granting Arjuna's value system); Ramanuja reads it as niṣkāma-karma already (the symmetry holds only when action is detached from fruits). Both readings agree that 2.37 closes one argument and opens the next, with 2.38 stating the equanimity doctrine explicitly.
